notes: EVA-5 pilot eval set. Each prompt embeds the session narrative to mine (headless runs have no real conversation history — accepted pilot limitation). Prompts are identical across with_skill/without_skill arms; the with-skill runner prepends only the skill-path preamble. Assertions are graded programmatically by goals/EVA-5-skill-creator-guidelines-evals/evals/grade_runs.py.

evals.name: goal-folder-quick
evals.prompt:
We're pausing here and a fresh session will pick this up, so capture everything from this working session in a handoff document. Create the handoff file in this repo wherever it belongs, structured so the next session can resume immediately without re-deriving anything.

Recap of the session you are handing off (this is the material to mine — treat it as the session history):

We worked the rate-limiter goal (goals/ABC-9-rate-limiter-tuning/). In src/limiter.ts we raised DEFAULT_BURST from 10 to 25 and extracted the refill logic out of consume() into a standalone refill() function. In src/limiter.test.ts we added six jitter tests. Test suite currently: 12/14 passing — the two failures are both in 'jitter under load', timer drift of roughly 40ms. We first tried a sliding-window log implementation but rejected it: at 10k clients it needs ~80MB versus ~2MB for the token bucket. We also rejected a fixed-window counter because it allows burst spikes at window boundaries; final decision is token bucket with lazy refill. Along the way we discovered vitest fake timers do not advance performance.now() unless the shim option is enabled — that's exactly what the two failing tests trip over. I told you mid-session: 'stop adding config knobs — hardcode burst to 25 for now, we'll revisit'. Next steps we agreed: fix the two jitter tests using the vitest clock shim, then wire the limiter into the gateway middleware. Still open: whether the gateway needs per-route buckets or one global bucket.
evals.expected_output: A handoff markdown file under goals/ABC-9-rate-limiter-tuning/handoffs/ named handoff-<YYYYMMDD-HHmmSS>-<slug>.md, following the handoff template: chain header block (chain tag ABC-9-rate-limiter-tuning, seq 1, no parent), all required sections including User Feedback & Preferences and Self-Check, concrete numbers preserved (12/14, 40ms, 80MB vs 2MB), at or above the 150-line Quick floor.
expectations: A handoff file exists at goals/ABC-9-rate-limiter-tuning/handoffs/ named handoff-<timestamp>-<slug>.md
The header block records the chain tag ABC-9-rate-limiter-tuning at seq 1
The header block records no parent (first in chain)

All required template sections are present (The Goal, Where We Are, What We Tried, Key Decisions, Evidence & Data, Files Changed, User Feedback & Preferences, Where We're Going, Risks & Blockers, Open Questions, Quick Start, Self-Check)
The 12/14 test measurement is preserved verbatim
The 'hardcode burst to 25 / stop adding config knobs' user correction is captured
The memory comparison (80MB vs 2MB) survives with real numbers
The file is at least 150 lines (Quick-pass floor)
The Self-Check section records the pass choice, line count, gap pass, completeness, and deliberate omissions

evals.name: standalone-fallback
evals.prompt:
We're stopping for the day and a fresh session will pick this up, so capture everything from this working session in a handoff document. Create the handoff file in this repo wherever it belongs, structured so the next session can resume immediately without re-deriving anything.

Recap of the session you are handing off (this is the material to mine — treat it as the session history):

This was exploratory debugging, not attached to any tracked goal. We investigated the flaky integration-test timeouts on CI: 3 of the last 40 runs failed. Root cause: two CI jobs landing on the same runner collide on the shared Postgres container's fixed host port. Fix implemented in tests/integration/test-setup.ts — allocate an ephemeral port and retry container startup once; after the fix we got 25 consecutive green runs. We considered and rejected simply raising the test timeout from 30s to 120s: it masks the collision instead of fixing it and would slow every legitimate failure. You told me: 'don't touch the CI yaml, fix it in the test harness'. Remaining work: backport the fix to the release/2.3 branch, and check whether the nightly perf job uses the same shared container pattern.
evals.expected_output: A handoff markdown file under .claude/handoffs/ (repo-root fallback — no goal/loop folder exists) named handoff-<YYYYMMDD-HHmmSS>-<slug>.md, chain tag standalone-<hex> at seq 1, all required sections including User Feedback & Preferences and Self-Check, concrete numbers preserved (3 of 40, 25 consecutive, 30s->120s), at or above the 150-line Quick floor.
expectations: A handoff file exists under .claude/handoffs/ (the no-goal fallback location) with the handoff-<timestamp>-<slug>.md naming
The chain tag is a standalone-<hex> tag (no goal folder exists to name the chain)
The header block records seq 1 with no parent
All required template sections are present including User Feedback & Preferences and Self-Check
The flake measurement (3 of the last 40 runs) is preserved
The verification measurement (25 consecutive green runs) is preserved
The rejected alternative (raising timeout 30s to 120s) is captured with its rejection rationale
The 'don't touch the CI yaml' user constraint is captured
The file is at least 150 lines (Quick-pass floor)

evals.name: chain-continuation
evals.prompt:
Context is running low, so before we stop, capture everything from this working session in a handoff document. Create the handoff file in this repo wherever it belongs, structured so the next session can resume immediately without re-deriving anything.

Recap of the session you are handing off (this is the material to mine — treat it as the session history):

We continued the cache-layer goal (goals/ABC-7-cache-layer/) — this session picked up directly from the previous session's handoff in that goal's handoffs/ folder. What we did: implemented the TTL eviction tests that handoff called for — nine new tests in src/cache/ttl.test.ts, suite now 31/31 passing. We also renamed warmCacheOnBoot() to preloadCache() and moved it from the draft location into src/cache/preload.ts. We benchmarked the eviction sweep: 4.2ms per sweep with the naive scan, 0.8ms after switching to a lazy min-heap keyed on expiry — that heap approach is what we kept. Rejected: a periodic full-scan timer (simpler but O(n) every tick regardless of load). Nothing else from the previous plan was started. Open: whether preloadCache() should block readiness or warm in the background.
evals.expected_output: A second handoff in goals/ABC-7-cache-layer/handoffs/ continuing the chain: parent set to the existing handoff-20260712-091500-cache-layer.md, seq 2, a Since Last Handoff section comparing the parent's plan to what happened, a Stale References section flagging CacheMetricsReporter (named in the parent but absent from the codebase), all required sections including Self-Check, at or above the 150-line floor.
expectations: A new handoff file is created in goals/ABC-7-cache-layer/handoffs/ alongside the existing parent
The header block names handoff-20260712-091500-cache-layer.md as parent at seq 2
A Since Last Handoff section is present (parent plan vs what actually happened)
A Stale References section flags CacheMetricsReporter, which the parent references but no longer resolves in the codebase
The 31/31 test measurement is preserved
The eviction-sweep benchmark (4.2ms to 0.8ms) is preserved with both numbers
All required template sections are present including User Feedback & Preferences and Self-Check
The file is at least 150 lines (Quick-pass floor)
